SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper runs extensive experiments across 15 datasets in four categories, compares a wide range of supervised (linear and non-linear) models, and explicitly varies available training data to measure data-efficiency—these choices give strong empirical support for the main claims. They also discuss limitations (e.g., computational cost of the regression NN for analogy and excluding MSR/Google analogy sets due to overfitting) and provide code and related-work coverage. However, hyperparameters are not reported and there is limited detail on sensitivity/ablation beyond the training-size and model-type variations, which reduces full reproducibility and leaves some minor gaps in analysis. | B(4): The experimental setup is strong: embeddings are evaluated across 15 diverse datasets and with a wide range of supervised models while explicitly varying training-data sizes to measure data efficiency, which supports the main claims about task-dependent rankings and linear vs non-linear recoverability. However, hyperparameters are not reported (hindering reproducibility) and the paper notes computational limitations for the regression neural network for analogy, indicating some practical gaps. Related work coverage is described as adequate and some limitations (overfitting on certain analogy sets) are discussed, but explicit ablation/sensitivity analyses beyond varying dataset size and no full hyperparameter sensitivity study are missing.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper runs extensive experiments across 15 datasets in four categories, compares a wide range of supervised (linear and non-linear) models, and explicitly varies available training data to measure data-efficiency—these choices give strong empirical support for the main claims. They also discuss limitations (e.g., computational cost of the regression NN for analogy and excluding MSR/Google analogy sets due to overfitting) and provide code and related-work coverage. However, hyperparameters are not reported and there is limited detail on sensitivity/ablation beyond the training-size and model-type variations, which reduces full reproducibility and leaves some minor gaps in analysis.

reviewer_b.justification: The experimental setup is strong: embeddings are evaluated across 15 diverse datasets and with a wide range of supervised models while explicitly varying training-data sizes to measure data efficiency, which supports the main claims about task-dependent rankings and linear vs non-linear recoverability. However, hyperparameters are not reported (hindering reproducibility) and the paper notes computational limitations for the regression neural network for analogy, indicating some practical gaps. Related work coverage is described as adequate and some limitations (overfitting on certain analogy sets) are discussed, but explicit ablation/sensitivity analyses beyond varying dataset size and no full hyperparameter sensitivity study are missing.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The topic—evaluation of word embeddings with a focus on data efficiency and supervised tasks—is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues and addresses a core audience interested in representation evaluation. The paper uses methods and evaluation standards expected at such venues (15 datasets across similarity, analogy, sentence and single-word tasks; a range of linear and non-linear supervised models; comparative baselines; code release). Minor concerns for a top-tier submission are the omitted hyperparameter reporting and the exclusion of large analogy sets (MSR/Google) due to overfitting and computational costs, which could limit reproducibility and completeness but do not undermine overall relevance. | B(5): Yes — this is a strong fit for a top NLP/ML venue. The paper tackles an important empirical/methodological question for the NLP community (evaluation of word embeddings) and proposes practical supervised evaluation protocols measuring data efficiency across 15 standard datasets (Similarity, Analogy, Sentence, Single-word) using a range of linear and non-linear models. The scope, datasets, and baselines (e.g., cosine, 3COSADD/3COSMUL, RF, SVM, CNN) are appropriate for top conferences, and the availability of code and adequate related-work coverage further support suitability for a high-tier venue.

reviewer_a.justification: The topic—evaluation of word embeddings with a focus on data efficiency and supervised tasks—is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues and addresses a core audience interested in representation evaluation. The paper uses methods and evaluation standards expected at such venues (15 datasets across similarity, analogy, sentence and single-word tasks; a range of linear and non-linear supervised models; comparative baselines; code release). Minor concerns for a top-tier submission are the omitted hyperparameter reporting and the exclusion of large analogy sets (MSR/Google) due to overfitting and computational costs, which could limit reproducibility and completeness but do not undermine overall relevance.

The scope, datasets, and baselines (e.g., cosine, 3COSADD/3COSMUL, RF, SVM, CNN) are appropriate for top conferences, and the availability of code and adequate related-work coverage further support suitability for a high-tier venue.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are experimentally grounded: a broad set of 15 datasets and a range of supervised (linear and non-linear) models were used and training-data sweep analyses plausibly support the assertions about data-efficiency and model non-linearity differences between embeddings. There are no formal mathematical derivations to check, and none appear necessary for the empirical claims. However, methodological shortcomings reduce reproducibility and robustness: hyperparameters were not reported and there is no mention of statistical significance testing, and the paper notes some practical limitations (e.g. expensive regression NN for analogy). These are minor but meaningful issues that do not overturn the primary experimental conclusions. | B(4): The paper's central claims are largely supported by extensive experiments across 15 diverse datasets using both linear and non-linear supervised models and by explicitly varying training-set size to measure data efficiency; they also use appropriate baselines (cosine, analogy methods) and avoid obvious data-leakage (omitting MSR/Google analogy sets). However, important methodological details are missing: hyperparameters are not reported and there is no mention of statistical significance testing, which weakens reproducibility and confidence in some comparisons. Overall the experimental design and results justify the conclusions, but the missing experimental details constitute a minor but material limitation.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims are experimentally grounded: a broad set of 15 datasets and a range of supervised (linear and non-linear) models were used and training-data sweep analyses plausibly support the assertions about data-efficiency and model non-linearity differences between embeddings. There are no formal mathematical derivations to check, and none appear necessary for the empirical claims. However, methodological shortcomings reduce reproducibility and robustness: hyperparameters were not reported and there is no mention of statistical significance testing, and the paper notes some practical limitations (e.g. expensive regression NN for analogy). These are minor but meaningful issues that do not overturn the primary experimental conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's central claims are largely supported by extensive experiments across 15 diverse datasets using both linear and non-linear supervised models and by explicitly varying training-set size to measure data efficiency; they also use appropriate baselines (cosine, analogy methods) and avoid obvious data-leakage (omitting MSR/Google analogy sets). However, important methodological details are missing: hyperparameters are not reported and there is no mention of statistical significance testing, which weakens reproducibility and confidence in some comparisons. Overall the experimental design and results justify the conclusions, but the missing experimental details constitute a minor but material limitation.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper advances a clear, new perspective by prioritizing data-efficiency and simple supervised tasks for embedding evaluation—specifically by varying available training data and reporting supervised model scores on subsets using both linear and non-linear models. This contrasts with common unsupervised cosine/analogy evaluations and is shown to reveal differences (e.g., GloVe100 vs GloVe300) and non-linearly encoded information that cosine metrics can miss. While related supervised/probing evaluations exist, the explicit focus on data-efficiency trajectories and model non-linearity as primary evaluation axes is a notable and clearly differentiated contribution rather than a trivial recombination of prior work. | B(4): The paper introduces a notable new perspective by arguing evaluation should emphasize data efficiency and simple supervised tasks (varying training data and reporting performance trajectories), which is a meaningful shift from standard unsupervised cosine/analogy metrics. The empirical finding that similarity and analogy information is often non-linearly encoded and thus missed by cosine-based evaluations further differentiates it from prior work. However, the overall problem of embedding evaluation is well-studied and the methods (supervised models, learning-curve style analysis across 15 datasets) are extensions of existing techniques rather than wholly new algorithms, so the contribution is notable but not revolutionary.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper advances a clear, new perspective by prioritizing data-efficiency and simple supervised tasks for embedding evaluation—specifically by varying available training data and reporting supervised model scores on subsets using both linear and non-linear models. This contrasts with common unsupervised cosine/analogy evaluations and is shown to reveal differences (e.g., GloVe100 vs GloVe300) and non-linearly encoded information that cosine metrics can miss. While related supervised/probing evaluations exist, the explicit focus on data-efficiency trajectories and model non-linearity as primary evaluation axes is a notable and clearly differentiated contribution rather than a trivial recombination of prior work.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper introduces a notable new perspective by arguing evaluation should emphasize data efficiency and simple supervised tasks (varying training data and reporting performance trajectories), which is a meaningful shift from standard unsupervised cosine/analogy metrics. The empirical finding that similarity and analogy information is often non-linearly encoded and thus missed by cosine-based evaluations further differentiates it from prior work. However, the overall problem of embedding evaluation is well-studied and the methods (supervised models, learning-curve style analysis across 15 datasets) are extensions of existing techniques rather than wholly new algorithms, so the contribution is notable but not revolutionary.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a broad and appropriate set of baselines (unsupervised cosine, 3COSADD/3COSMUL and a range of supervised models like RF, SVR, SVM, CNN, k-NN, regression NN) across 15 datasets and common metrics, and analyzes differences (data-efficiency, linear vs non-linear suitability) with concrete examples (e.g. GloVe100 vs GloVe300). Comparisons appear broadly fair (same datasets and varying training set sizes), but hyperparameters are not reported and some standard analogy sets (MSR/Google) were excluded, which are minor fairness/coverage omissions. Related work coverage is described as adequate, but the summary does not clearly state whether the paper explicitly acknowledges when prior methods perform comparably or better. | B(4): The paper compares against standard unsupervised baselines (cosine, 3COSADD/3COSMUL) and a wide range of supervised models (RF, SVR-RBF, regression NN, SVM-RBF, CNN, k-NN, Naive Bayes, etc.) across the same datasets and metrics while reporting performance as training-data subsets (data-efficiency). Related work coverage is noted as adequate and the analysis highlights differences (e.g., GloVe100 vs GloVe300) along useful axes (data-efficiency, linear vs non-linear). Minor issues reduce full marks: hyperparameters are not reported (which can affect fairness) and the summary does not explicitly state when prior methods sometimes perform comparably or better.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a broad and appropriate set of baselines (unsupervised cosine, 3COSADD/3COSMUL and a range of supervised models like RF, SVR, SVM, CNN, k-NN, regression NN) across 15 datasets and common metrics, and analyzes differences (data-efficiency, linear vs non-linear suitability) with concrete examples (e.g. GloVe100 vs GloVe300). Comparisons appear broadly fair (same datasets and varying training set sizes), but hyperparameters are not reported and some standard analogy sets (MSR/Google) were excluded, which are minor fairness/coverage omissions. Related work coverage is described as adequate, but the summary does not clearly state whether the paper explicitly acknowledges when prior methods perform comparably or better.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against standard unsupervised baselines (cosine, 3COSADD/3COSMUL) and a wide range of supervised models (RF, SVR-RBF, regression NN, SVM-RBF, CNN, k-NN, Naive Bayes, etc.) across the same datasets and metrics while reporting performance as training-data subsets (data-efficiency). Related work coverage is noted as adequate and the analysis highlights differences (e.g., GloVe100 vs GloVe300) along useful axes (data-efficiency, linear vs non-linear). Minor issues reduce full marks: hyperparameters are not reported (which can affect fairness) and the summary does not explicitly state when prior methods sometimes perform comparably or better.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important problem—how to evaluate word embeddings—by proposing data-efficiency and simple supervised-task-focused evaluations applied across 15 datasets and a range of linear and non-linear models. Its demonstration that similarity/analogy information is often non-linearly encoded (so common cosine/analogy metrics can miss recoverable information) and that embedding rankings are task- and data-dependent (e.g., differing behavior of GloVe100 vs GloVe300) would likely change evaluation practice. Given the concrete protocol, diverse baselines/models, and available code, the work opens new research directions and is likely to be adopted by researchers, yielding a strong-impact assessment. | B(4): The paper tackles an important practical evaluation gap by proposing data-efficiency and simple supervised-task based evaluation across 15 datasets and many supervised models, which directly affects how embeddings are compared (e.g., showing GloVe100 vs GloVe300 differ in data-efficiency and linear vs non-linear suitability). Its findings that similarity/analogy information is often non-linearly encoded and that there is no universally best embedding would likely change researcher and practitioner evaluation practices and downstream model choices. The work opens clear new directions (designing embeddings for data-efficiency and model-type suitability) and is likely to be adopted or extended by others, giving it strong but not necessarily transformative impact.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—how to evaluate word embeddings—by proposing data-efficiency and simple supervised-task-focused evaluations applied across 15 datasets and a range of linear and non-linear models. Its demonstration that similarity/analogy information is often non-linearly encoded (so common cosine/analogy metrics can miss recoverable information) and that embedding rankings are task- and data-dependent (e.g., differing behavior of GloVe100 vs GloVe300) would likely change evaluation practice. Given the concrete protocol, diverse baselines/models, and available code, the work opens new research directions and is likely to be adopted by researchers, yielding a strong-impact assessment.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important practical evaluation gap by proposing data-efficiency and simple supervised-task based evaluation across 15 datasets and many supervised models, which directly affects how embeddings are compared (e.g., showing GloVe100 vs GloVe300 differ in data-efficiency and linear vs non-linear suitability). Its findings that similarity/analogy information is often non-linearly encoded and that there is no universally best embedding would likely change researcher and practitioner evaluation practices and downstream model choices. The work opens clear new directions (designing embeddings for data-efficiency and model-type suitability) and is likely to be adopted or extended by others, giving it strong but not necessarily transformative impact.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with clear main claims stated early (focus on data efficiency and simple supervised tasks) and a detailed methodology and experimental setup across 15 datasets using various supervised models. Notation and writing quality are reported as good, related work coverage is adequate, and code availability improves reproducibility. Presentation issues are minor: hyperparameters are not reported and a stated limitation (computationally intensive regression NN) suggests some experimental details or trade-offs are not fully documented. | B(4): The paper is overall well-organized and well-written: the methodology, experiments (datasets and baselines), and limitations are clearly laid out, and the main contributions are stated early in the summary. Related work coverage is adequate and writing quality is noted as good. Minor presentation issues remain — hyperparameters are not reported, and the summary does not confirm whether figures/tables or notation were fully detailed, which slightly limits reproducibility and completeness of presentation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears well-organized with clear main claims stated early (focus on data efficiency and simple supervised tasks) and a detailed methodology and experimental setup across 15 datasets using various supervised models. Notation and writing quality are reported as good, related work coverage is adequate, and code availability improves reproducibility. Presentation issues are minor: hyperparameters are not reported and a stated limitation (computationally intensive regression NN) suggests some experimental details or trade-offs are not fully documented.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is overall well-organized and well-written: the methodology, experiments (datasets and baselines), and limitations are clearly laid out, and the main contributions are stated early in the summary. Related work coverage is adequate and writing quality is noted as good. Minor presentation issues remain — hyperparameters are not reported, and the summary does not confirm whether figures/tables or notation were fully detailed, which slightly limits reproducibility and completeness of presentation.

main_claims: Evaluation of word embeddings should focus on data efficiency and simple supervised tasks, varying available training data and reporting supervised model scores for subsets.

The proposed supervised evaluation (including linear and non-linear models) reveals differences between embeddings along data efficiency and model non-linearity dimensions, offering insights not seen in purely unsupervised cosine-based evaluations.
Information about word similarity and analogy is often non-linearly encoded in embeddings, so commonly used unsupervised cosine/analogy metrics can miss recoverable information.

There are no universally good embeddings; embedding rankings are task dependent and embeddings differ in data-efficiency and suitability for linear vs non-linear downstream models (example: differing behavior of GloVe100 vs GloVe300).
_summary.methodology: Evaluate pretrained word embeddings on 15 datasets across four categories (Similarity, Analogy, Sentence, Single word) using a range of supervised models (linear and non-linear), vary the amount of training data to measure data efficiency and report performance/rank trajectories for each subset.
datasets: POS tagging datasets (verb, noun, adjective) from Faruqui and Dyer lexicons
Word sentiment (from Faruqui and Dyer lexicons)
Word color association (from Faruqui and Dyer lexicons)
WordNet synset membership (2 datasets from Faruqui and Dyer lexicons)
baselines: Support Vector Regression (SVR) with RBF kernel
Support Vector Machine (SVM) with RBF kernel
limitations: Regression neural network approach for analogy is very computationally intensive.
Models easily overfit when train and test sets share the same words (not used MSR and Google analogy datasets for this reason).
